content:
# James Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 264-265 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_005_page_001.jpg, JAS_005_page_002.jpg

**1** Now listen, you wealthy people — weep and wail over the miseries that are about to overtake you. **2** Your riches have rotted away, and your fine clothes have been eaten by moths. **3** Your gold and silver have corroded, and their corrosion will testify against you and consume your flesh like fire. You have hoarded wealth in the last days!

**4** Look! The wages you withheld from the workers who harvested your fields are crying out against you, and the cries of those harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of Hosts. **5** You have lived on the earth in luxury and self-indulgence. You have fattened yourselves for the day of slaughter. **6** You have condemned and murdered the innocent person, who does not resist you.

**7** So be patient, brothers and sisters, until the Lord comes. See how the farmer waits for the precious harvest from the land, being patient for it until it receives the early and the late rains. **8** You too must be patient. Stand firm in your hearts, because the Lord's coming is near. **9** Do not grumble against one another, brothers and sisters, so that you will not be judged. Look — the Judge is standing right at the door!

**10** Brothers and sisters, take the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord as an example of patience in the face of suffering. **11** See how we consider blessed those who have endured. You have heard about the perseverance of Job, and you have seen the outcome the Lord brought about — because the Lord is deeply compassionate and merciful.

**12** Above all, my brothers and sisters, do not swear — not by heaven, not by earth, not by any other oath. Simply let your "Yes" be yes and your "No" be no, so that you will not fall under judgment.

**13** Is anyone among you suffering? They should pray. Is anyone in good spirits? They should sing songs of praise. **14** Is anyone among you sick? They should call for the elders of the church, and the elders should pray over them, anointing them with oil in the name of the Lord. **15** And the prayer offered in faith will restore the sick person, and the Lord will raise them up. If they have committed sins, they will be forgiven. **16** Therefore, confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful and produces wonderful results.

**17** Elijah was a human being just like us. He prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain on the land for three years and six months. **18** Then he prayed again, and the sky poured down rain and the earth produced its crops.

**19** My brothers and sisters, if any one of you wanders away from the truth and someone brings them back, **20** understand this: whoever turns a sinner from the error of their ways will save that person's soul from death and will cover over a multitude of sins.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "weep and wail":** The Greek *klausate ololyzontes* pairs weeping with a howling cry of lamentation. The verb *ololyzein* is a raw, visceral sound of grief found in prophetic judgment oracles. This is not quiet sorrow but public anguish at impending catastrophe.

**Verse 3 — "corroded... consume your flesh like fire":** Sinaiticus reads *katiotai* (has corroded) and *phagetai tas sarkas hymon hos pyr* (will eat your flesh like fire). The image is deliberately gruesome: the very corrosion of hoarded wealth becomes a devouring agent. The phrase "in the last days" (*en eschatais hemerais*) gives the whole warning its eschatological urgency — they are stockpiling treasure when the age is ending.

**Verse 4 — "Lord of Hosts":** The Greek *Kyriou Sabaoth* preserves the Hebrew divine title untranslated, evoking the God of armies who fights for the oppressed. This is one of only two New Testament uses of this title (cf. Rom 9:29), and it deliberately invokes Old Testament prophetic fury against economic injustice.

**Verse 5 — "fattened yourselves for the day of slaughter":** The Greek *ethrepsate tas kardias hymon en hemera sphages* carries a devastating irony — the wealthy have been feeding themselves like livestock being prepared for butchering, oblivious that their indulgence is fattening them for judgment.

**Verse 6 — "the innocent person, who does not resist you":** Sinaiticus reads *ouk antitassetai hymin* (does not resist you). The righteous person's nonresistance heightens the moral outrage. Some scholars see a veiled reference to Christ himself, though the primary meaning is the vulnerable poor who lack legal recourse.

**Verse 7 — "the early and the late rains":** The Greek *proimon kai opsimon* refers to the autumn rains that soften the ground for planting and the spring rains that bring the harvest to maturity. This is deeply rooted in Palestinian agricultural life and expresses the long patience required between sowing and reaping.

**Verse 9 — "the Judge is standing right at the door":** The Greek *pro ton thyron hesteken* (stands before the doors) conveys breathtaking imminence. The judge is not approaching or on the way — he has already arrived and stands at the threshold. This vivid image echoes Jesus' own teaching (Mark 13:29).

**Verse 11 — "deeply compassionate and merciful":** The Greek *polysplagchnos... kai oiktirmon* — the first word, *polysplagchnos* (much-boweled, deeply feeling in one's gut), is exceptionally rare and may be a Jamesian coinage. It describes compassion that is visceral, not merely intellectual. The dynamic rendering "deeply compassionate" attempts to recover this intensity.

**Verse 12 — "do not swear":** This prohibition against oaths (*mee omnuete*) closely parallels Jesus' teaching in Matthew 5:34-37. James is not forbidding all solemn speech but rather the casual invocation of sacred things to bolster one's credibility. Simple truthfulness should make oaths unnecessary.

**Verse 14 — "anointing them with oil":** The Greek *aleipsantes elaioo* uses the ordinary word for rubbing with oil (*aleiphein*), not the ceremonial or sacramental term (*chriein*). This suggests a practical, pastoral act of care — olive oil was common medicine in the ancient world — combined with prayer.

**Verse 15 — "the prayer offered in faith will restore the sick person":** The Greek *hee euchee tees pisteos sosei ton kamnonta* uses *sozein* (to save/heal/deliver), which carries both physical and spiritual dimensions. James deliberately leaves this ambiguity intact — God's healing work encompasses the whole person.

**Verse 17 — "a human being just like us":** The Greek *homoiopathees heemin* (of similar nature/experience to us) is a key theological move. James dismantles the tendency to elevate prophetic heroes into untouchable figures. Elijah's power came not from superhuman status but from earnest prayer — and that same resource is available to every believer.

**Verses 19-20 — "will save that person's soul from death":** The closing verses of James shift from instruction to mission. The Greek *sosei psycheen autou ek thanatou* (will save a soul from death) and *kalypsei plethos hamartion* (will cover a multitude of sins) echo Proverbs 10:12. The letter ends not with a farewell but with a commission — the community's task is mutual rescue.

**Sinaiticus-specific note:** The Sinaiticus text of James 5 begins in column 4 of page 264 (folio 264r) and concludes in column 2 of page 265 with the subscript title *IAKOBOU* (Of James). The text is generally well-preserved and closely follows the majority textual tradition. The scribe writes in the characteristic continuous uncial script. Minor orthographic variations include itacistic spellings typical of the fourth-century Alexandrian scribal tradition. Notably, Sinaiticus includes the full text of all 20 verses without significant omission or addition compared to the critical text.
